lens.included_activities: US lower-middle-market converters of tissue, towel, napkin, wipes, absorbent hygiene, and related sanitary paper products that repeatedly manufacture or supply private-label, contract, distributor, institutional, medical, janitorial, foodservice, or retail programs for external customers.
lens.excluded_activities: National consumer-brand businesses without an outsourced manufacturing relationship, captive converting plants, pulp and parent-roll mills outside the code, pure distributors, one-off product sellers, shells, and non-control financing vehicles.
lens.customer_and_revenue_model: Recurring per-unit product revenue through private-label programs, contract manufacturing, recurring distributor and institutional supply, retail replenishment, and specification-based commercial accounts; pricing commonly reflects product grade, volume, packaging, freight, and fiber inputs.
lens.deviation_from_default: The code mixes branded consumer-goods manufacturers with repeat outsourced contract/private-label converters and institutional suppliers. The lens keeps only the latter accountable recurring relationships because applying an outsourced-service screen to national-brand consumer sales would be incoherent.

a.bridge: Four-digit BLS data show paper-goods machine operators are a major converted-paper occupation, and O*NET describes their physical setup, machine-control, inspection, and adjustment tasks. A cross-economy GPT study supplies an upper-context benchmark for language-task exposure. I map low exposure to continuous physical converting, maintenance, packing, and material handling, and higher exposure to planning, quality records, forecasting, procurement, customer service, and administration. The generic GPT benchmark likely overstates exposure in this capital-intensive production mix.
a.rationale: Sanitary-paper converting is highly mechanized but still needs operators, technicians, material handling, inspection, and maintenance that generative AI does not directly replace. AI can reduce labor in demand planning, production scheduling, specification and artwork checks, order entry, procurement, service, compliance documentation, and predictive analytics. The range excludes conventional line automation already embedded in current operations.
a.change_evidence: A 322291-specific OEWS occupation-and-wage extract, combined with task-level time studies at at least ten private-label tissue and absorbent-product converters that separate existing line automation from incremental AI capability.
caveats: No public source measures wage-weighted not-yet-automated AI task exposure for NAICS 322291.
The BLS industry evidence is at four digits and includes other converted paper products.
Automation intensity varies materially between tissue converting, napkins, wipes, and absorbent hygiene products.

rho.bridge: A purpose-designed Census survey found 22.8% of US manufacturing plants used any industrial AI in 2021, while Federal Reserve monitoring of Census business surveys reports about 18% of firms had adopted AI by year-end 2025. Those are current adoption measures, not the five-year share of exposed opportunity operationally implemented. I project higher diffusion as packaged planning, vision, maintenance, and document tools mature, but discount for validation, hygiene quality, retailer specifications, legacy controls, and small-firm integration. Current broad adoption can overstate deep production use.
rho.rationale: Recurring high-volume production, machine telemetry, standardized SKUs, and measurable defects make targeted analytics and planning use cases implementable. Retailer qualification, change-control requirements, uptime risk, thin technical teams, cybersecurity, and integration with legacy ERP/MES and line controls prevent complete implementation.
rho.change_evidence: A 322291 plant survey of deployed AI use cases, pilot-to-production rates, machine-data coverage, validation requirements, exception rates, labor savings, and implementation cost by product family and plant scale.
caveats: Industrial AI includes non-generative technologies and shallow uses.
The evidence covers all manufacturing or all businesses, not LMM sanitary-paper converters.
Implementation may be faster in telemetry-rich tissue lines than in fragmented specialty converting.

e.rationale: The industry contains clear private-label and contract-manufacturing models for retailers, distributors, commercial users, and medical or institutional customers, but it also includes integrated national brands and captive plants outside the lens. Within the frozen LMM population, independent converters should be more prevalent than among the largest firms, yet eligibility still requires repeat external production accountability rather than mere branded sales.
e.change_evidence: Classify the 45 dataset-estimated LMM firms using Capital IQ, PitchBook, company websites, retailer supplier lists, and management interviews, measuring the share of revenue from private-label, contract, and recurring institutional programs.
caveats: Public statistics do not report contract/private-label revenue share at the firm level.
The frozen n estimate is margin-bridged and may misstate the LMM population.
Some firms combine eligible contract converting with excluded proprietary-brand revenue.

s5.bridge: Census reports that more than half of US business owners were age 55 or older, and BizBuySell reports active small-manufacturing transactions, including a 16% year-over-year increase in Q1 2026. These are broad succession and transaction signals rather than a 322291 five-year control-transfer rate. I translate them into a bounded five-year probability and discount closures, internal transfers, strategic minority investments, and firms that are too capital-intensive or customer-concentrated to transfer. Platform evidence may overstate liquidity by observing completed listings.
s5.rationale: Aging ownership and demonstrated manufacturing deal activity create a plausible transfer pipeline. Essential products and recurring customers aid financeability, but equipment capex, fiber volatility, retailer concentration, qualification risk, and large incumbent competition can narrow the buyer pool or lead owners to sell assets rather than going concerns.
s5.change_evidence: Five-year transaction logs from PitchBook, GF Data, Axial, BizBuySell, SBA 7(a) change-of-ownership loans, and paper-industry brokers, filtered to US sanitary-paper converters and reconciled to the eligible-firm denominator.
caveats: Owner-age data cover all US employer businesses, not this code or EBITDA band.
BizBuySell skews smaller than much of the target band and omits private transactions.
Plant or asset sales may not be qualifying control transfers of transferable firms.

q.rationale: Contract and private-label prices are typically fixed per unit or program for a period, allowing temporary retention of scheduling, scrap, downtime, and administrative savings. Retention is constrained by powerful retailers and distributors, competitive rebids, transparent fiber and freight adjustments, and high-volume procurement. Reliability, quality, reduced stockouts, and faster innovation are more defensible sources of capture than visible direct-labor cuts.
q.change_evidence: Review at least 30 customer contracts and bid histories for open-book costing, index clauses, renewal cadence, cost-down requirements, rebates, service penalties, and gross-margin changes following documented automation deployments.
caveats: No public source isolates pass-through of AI-related savings in sanitary-paper contracts.
Customer concentration can make realized retention substantially lower than unit-price form suggests.
Fiber, energy, freight, and packaging movements can obscure operational savings.

d5.bridge: Census reports US population grew 0.5% from July 2024 to July 2025 and projects that one in five Americans will be retirement age by 2030; CDC reports 3,628,934 births in 2024, up 1% from 2023, but a 1% decline in the general fertility rate and a record-low total fertility rate. These demographic quantities drive but do not directly measure sanitary-paper demand. I map modest population growth and aging to stable-to-growing tissue and adult-care demand, offset by low fertility for baby products, conservation, reusable alternatives, and mix shifts. Population proxies may overstate volume if per-capita tissue use falls.
d5.rationale: Bathroom tissue, towels, napkins, wipes, feminine care, and incontinence products are recurring necessities with limited digital substitution. Population growth and aging support the basket, while low fertility tempers diapers and sustainability pressures can reduce per-use material. Constant-quality demand should be roughly stable to modestly higher over five years, with substantial product-mix divergence.
d5.change_evidence: Five-year constant-volume shipment history and forecasts by 322291 product class from Census AIES/Economic Census, Circana or NIQ retail scanner data, away-from-home distributor volumes, and customer forecasts separated into tissue, towels, napkins, diapers, feminine care, and incontinence.
caveats: Demographics do not capture inventory cycles, per-capita usage, import substitution, or sustainability-driven material reduction.
Birth trends are relevant only to a subset of the industry.
Aging supports adult incontinence but not necessarily paper-based products classified entirely within 322291.

o.rationale: Nearly all surviving demand requires physical conversion, packaging, quality control, and delivery by an accountable manufacturer. Software can automate ordering, forecasting, artwork, and service but cannot provide tissue or absorbent products. A small share could migrate to reusable substitutes, direct imports, customer vertical integration, or self-service procurement that removes the lens operator.
o.change_evidence: Customer procurement interviews and win/loss data quantifying shifts to reusable systems, direct imports, vertically integrated supply, or private-label platforms that eliminate independent converter accountability rather than merely changing vendors.
caveats: The estimate is conditional on year-five demand and does not duplicate demand loss captured in d5.
Accountability may consolidate into larger integrated producers even though physical production remains necessary.
Product classification of some nonwoven hygiene substitutes may fall outside this NAICS code.

narrative.executive_view: The coherent target is an independent private-label or contract converter with recurring retailer, distributor, institutional, or medical programs. Demand for the physical basket is durable, but AI acts mainly on planning, quality, commercial, and administrative workflows because direct converting is already mechanized and physically constrained.
narrative.ai_impact: AI can improve demand forecasting, scheduling, changeovers, predictive maintenance, visual inspection support, procurement, specification management, artwork checks, order entry, service, and compliance records. It will not by itself replace most line operation, maintenance, packing, or material movement.
narrative.value_capture: Per-unit program pricing preserves savings between bids, but concentrated retailers and distributors can demand cost-downs at renewal. Operators should expect better retention from uptime, service, quality, and stockout reduction than from transparent labor removal alone.
narrative.firm_availability: Independent contract and private-label converters clearly exist, while the broader code also contains large branded and captive operations. Aging ownership and active manufacturing transactions support deal flow, but the eligible LMM denominator is small and asset intensity may restrict transferability.
narrative.demand_durability: Sanitary tissue and hygiene products are recurring physical necessities. Slow population growth, aging, and adult-care needs support demand; record-low fertility and reusable or lighter-weight alternatives create offsets. Product mix matters more than digital substitution.
narrative.risks_and_uncertainty: Key risks are retailer concentration, fiber and energy volatility, capex, equipment reliability, quality failures, imports, environmental claims, and the limited depth of AI-exposed wages. A target dependent on one private-label customer, with obsolete lines or mandatory annual cost-downs, could be unattractive despite resilient end demand.
narrative.principal_driver: Essential repeat consumption and private-label programs create durable recurring production relationships.
narrative.principal_weakness: Capital-intensive physical operations leave only a minority of current labor tasks open to incremental AI substitution.

sources.title: NAICS 322291 Sanitary Paper Product Manufacturing
sources.supports: Defines the industry as converting purchased sanitary stock or wadding into tissues, napkins, toilet paper, towels, diapers, sanitary napkins, tampons, and related products.

sources.title: Converted Paper Product Manufacturing - May 2023 OEWS Industry-Specific Estimates
sources.supports: Shows paper-goods machine setters/operators/tenders at 63,660 workers and 23.32% of employment in the broader converted-paper industry.

sources.title: O*NET: Paper Goods Machine Setters, Operators, and Tenders
sources.supports: Documents physical machine setup, control, product inspection, and production adjustment tasks central to paper converting.

sources.title: GPTs are GPTs: An Early Look at the Labor Market Impact Potential of Large Language Models
sources.supports: Reports broad US workforce task-exposure benchmarks: about 80% with at least 10% of tasks affected and 19% with at least 50%; used only as an upper-context proxy.

sources.title: The Adoption of Industrial AI in America
sources.supports: Reports that 22.8% of approximately 28,500 US manufacturing establishments used any industrial AI in 2021, with lower intensity-weighted adoption.

sources.title: Monitoring AI Adoption in the US Economy
sources.supports: Reports from Census business surveys that about 18% of firms had adopted AI by year-end 2025.

sources.title: Select Products Holdings - Private Label Tissue and Paper Towel Manufacturer
sources.supports: Documents a US independent converter supplying retailers, distributors, and commercial partners with napkins, tissue, towels, branded offerings, and customized private-label programs.

sources.title: Global Tissue Group Contract Manufacturing
sources.supports: Documents contract manufacturing and private-brand tissue supply for national brands and integrated manufacturers, confirming the recurring outsourced production model.

sources.title: US Population Growth Slows Due to Historic Decline in Net International Migration
sources.supports: Reports US population grew by 1.8 million, or 0.5%, between July 2024 and July 2025.

sources.title: Demographic Turning Points for the United States: Population Projections for 2020 to 2060
sources.supports: Projects that one in five Americans will be retirement age by 2030 and that the population will continue growing, though more slowly.

sources.title: Annual Business Survey Visualizations
sources.supports: Census states that more than half of US business owners were age 55 or older in the 2019 Annual Business Survey.

sources.title: BizBuySell Insight Report - Market Trends
sources.supports: Reports Q1 2026 manufacturing acquisitions rose 16% year over year and 22% quarter over quarter, evidencing a selected small-manufacturing transaction market.

sources.title: Births: Final Data for 2024
sources.supports: Reports 3,628,934 US births in 2024, up 1% from 2023, while the general fertility rate fell 1% and the total fertility rate reached a record low.
